Article: did you expect the rebuild of evangelion
[]: did you expect the rebuild of evangelion?             Quote Post by whiemeat » Mon Aug 12, 2013 5:10 pm When I was 13 I shopped around the nearest pawn America looking merely for a copy of Appleseed (yes the 2004 one) I unfortunately didn't find one but I did see NGE: the perfect collection at that age when you see a box set you buy it then I went home and when I did I checked the release dates and all that jazz I was astounded to see that it was from the 90's I watched the entire series in two weeks with a.. humph that was good too bad there's no more after about a week I seen that there was a few movies of course I watched them both when I had the chance and wow all this from 95-97 and its done but a mere 2 years later I start to see you are not (alone) is coming out in Japan

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Mon Aug 12, 2013 5:21 pm I did, more or less. I was aware as early as 2004 that Hideaki Anno had been considering a sequel series.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Aug 12, 2013 6:42 pm I was new to anime in general when I first saw NGE in 2009. (It was only the 3rd or 4th anime title I had seen at the time.) I was really liking what I saw. So half way through it, I did some research on Evangelion, just to see how much of the series there was left to watch. I saw that after the TV series there was 2 movies completing it, then 4 proposed movies, 2 of them had already been made. To be honest, my heart sank. I assumed this meant that Eva was gonna be like Gundum, an anime I had intentionally avoided because I didn't want to have to sit through literally hundreds of episodes and thousands of hours of continuity and canon in order to just watch a TV show. I didn't want Evangelion to span many generations with different protagonists piloting Evas. This show worked way too well as a character study to suddenly turn it into a vessel for mindless action scenes featuring stock anime characters. I seriously thought about dropping the whole show right then and there. But I kept watching for

Article: did you expect the rebuild of evangelion
 piloting Evas. This show worked way too well as a character study to suddenly turn it into a vessel for mindless action scenes featuring stock anime characters. I seriously thought about dropping the whole show right then and there. But I kept watching for some reason. Eva's like that; once you start you can't ever stop, no matter what your opinion of the show might be at the time. (I think that's one of the show's greatest strengths.) So, I was surprised when the series most definitely ended with 25, 26, and EoE. There was absolutely no room for a sequel after that. I mean, now what? On a superficial level, all of your Eva Units are either dead or lost in space, Nerv and Seele don't exist in human form anymore, Rei somehow became a 100-mile high goddess before breaking into a million pieces, and, oh yeah, sans Asuka and Shinji all of humanity is now stew in the oceans. What's your next trick for the other 4 movies, guys? Is Asuka gonna merge with Kyoko and start swallowing entire galaxies? Knowing that it would be impossible in this continuity for Asuka to do such a thing, I went back and read up on the new "Rebuild" movies and found out that they were remakes instead of continuations. (Yeah, I know people are still debating that. But whatever. If entire galaxies aren't being swallowed in FINAL, then it fails at being a sequel simply based on the idea that sequels need to be bigger and bolder than the first installment.) What made me more excited was that it was the same creative team making the remakes. Cecil B. DeMille and Alfred Hitchcock did the same thing with a few of their selected works, and they managed to turn out a product better than their first time at telling the story. Since no one knew the works better than these guys, I can't imagine then screwing it up. And so far I've been enjoying these newer movies. It definitely has the same spirit and core appeal of the original show (especially with Q) while also managing to be a completely different story told in a bit more emotionally stable way than the first one. (Even though the emotional instability of the NGE is one of the reasons why I like it so much, a more stable approach to NTE doesn't cause it to lose anything vital to the franchise.)
